coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots from biorefinery waste for tri-channel sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ruibin
Jiao, Liang
Zhou, Xuelian
Guo, Ziyu
Bian, Huiyang
Dai, Hongqi
coredata.dc:description: Renewable lignocellulosic biomass can be effectively transformed to value-added products, enabling fast growth of related downstream processing. However, valorization of the by-produced cellulose-poor fraction, which is also in large volumes, is only occasionally reported regarding existing technologies. Here, a simple, general, and effective strategy for fabricating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from the Miscanthus (MC) biorefinery waste consisting of sugars and depolymerized lignin, is developed. This process involves the fast and selective removal of most lignin and hemicellulose based on mild acid hydrotrope fractionation, with followed hydrothermal carbonization. The as-fabricated MC-derived GQDs (M-GQDs) exhibit several advantages such as few-layer graphene-like single crystalline structure, sulfur and nitrogen co-doping, bright fluorescence, excitation-dependent photoluminescence, and long fluorescence lifetime (11.95 ns). Furthermore, M-GQDs present prominent fluorescence reduction in the presence of Fe3+ with good linearity (≤0.995) and very low detection limit (≥1.41 nM). Later, it is found that the observed high sensitivity for Fe3+ is based on a dynamic quenching mechanism, which is caused by the Fe3+-induced increase in both the energy dissipation and photogenerated electron consumption. This work is anticipated to open new opportunities for promoting the integral valorization of biomass and sensitive fluorometric detection of Fe3+.

(a) FTIR, (b) Raman and (c) XPS survey spectra of M-CQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of M-CQDs with regard to (d) S 2p, (e) C 1s, (f) N 1s, and (g) O 1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and PL emission spectra of M-CQDs. (b) CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram of M-GQDs upon excitation at 358 nm. (c) Detailed PL emission spectra of M-CQDs at various excitation wavelengths. (d) Time-resolved PL spectrum of M-CQDs with the fitted data shown in the inset.

PL emission/excitation spectra of M-GQDs (3 mgL–1) in the (a) absence and (b) presence of 500 μM of Fe3+ at different pH values. (c) Observations of M-GQDs (3 mgL–1) without (right) or mixed with (left) 500 μM of Fe3+ at pH 7, under day light (top) and 365 nm UV. The effect of different Fe3+ concentrations on (d) the emission spectrum excited under 358 nm, (e) the emission spectrum excited under 408 nm, and (f) the excitation spectrum under 358 nm, of M-CQDs (From top to bottom: 0–3000 μM). The Stern-Volmer plots calculated from (d)/(e)/(f) include two consecutive sections with high linear coefficient: (1) 0–10.6 μM of Fe3+ (g/h/i) and (2) 10.6–900/800/1000 μM of Fe3+ (j/k/l).

(a) Time-resolved PL decay curves of M-GQDs (3 mgL–1) in presence of various concentrations of Fe3+. (b) The proposed tri-channel fluorescence quenching mechanism in this study. The differential fluorescence quenching study of Fe3+ and various metal ions regarding their PL spectra of (c) Em 358 nm, (d) Em 408 nm, and (e) Ex 358 nm.

(a) The schematic diagram of preparation of M-GQDs via a two-step method includes the acid hydrotrope fractionation of MC and hydrothermal treatment. head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots from biorefinery waste for tri-channel sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Renewable lignocellulosic biomass can be effectively transformed to value-added products, enabling fast growth of related downstream processing. However, valorization of the by-produced cellulose-poor fraction, which is also in large volumes, is only occasionally reported regarding existing technologies. Here, a simple, general, and effective strategy for fabricating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from the Miscanthus (MC) biorefinery waste consisting of sugars and depolymerized lignin, is developed. This process involves the fast and selective removal of most lignin and hemicellulose based on mild acid hydrotrope fractionation, with followed hydrothermal carbonization. The as-fabricated MC-derived GQDs (M-GQDs) exhibit several advantages such as few-layer graphene-like single crystalline structure, sulfur and nitrogen co-doping, bright fluorescence, excitation-dependent photoluminescence, and long fluorescence lifetime (11.95 ns). Furthermore, M-GQDs present prominent fluorescence reduction in the presence of Fe3+ with good linearity (≤0.995) and very low detection limit (≥1.41 nM). Later, it is found that the observed high sensitivity for Fe3+ is based on a dynamic quenching mechanism, which is caused by the Fe3+-induced increase in both the energy dissipation and photogenerated electron consumption. This work is anticipated to open new opportunities for promoting the integral valorization of biomass and sensitive fluorometric detection of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:para: Physicochemical advantages such as tunable nanosize, ease of functionalization and long-term photostability have brought graphene quantum dots (GQDs) great attention for use in photoelectric conversion (Shen et al., 2012), heterogeneous catalysis (Li et al., 2018b), biomedicine (Kim et al., 2018), anti-counterfeiting (Bai et al., 2018), and sensitive indicating (Li et al., 2016), etc (Rahimi-Aghdam et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2015). Recently, fabrication of novel GQDs with intriguing properties from renewable and low-cost plant biomass has been a hot topic (Abbas et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018). As is well known, plant biomass is mainly composed of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin, the latter strongly contributes to the structural stability as well as chemical/bio-engineering recalcitrance (Wong et al., 2020; Ragauskas et al., 2014; Luo et al., 2016). In this case, some costly pre-treatments include microwave radiation (Zhang et al., 2016; Li et al., 2012a), chemical oxidation (Ding et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2017b), electrochemical treatment (Yang et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2017), and the integrated process (Temerov et al., 2019), are required to enable considerable performance. Besides, utilization of the major plant biomass feedstocks, especially the extensively studied agro-forestry residues, may entail some extra costs from harvesting/collecting, which severely hinders their further development.

To solve these issues, many efforts have been made by developing mild nonoxidative strategy, using ready-made industrial wastes with reduced recalcitrance, chemical doping, and compositing; for example, forming graphene-like single crystals based on the acid hydrotrope-induced depolymerization of alkali lignin (Wang et al., 2019), employing lignin-rich black liquor that is by-produced from industrial papermaking as a precursor (Jiang et al., 2019), and embedding nitrogen to acquire fluorescence enhancement (Ding et al., 2018). The advantages of the above strategies can be summarized as follows: (1) acid hydrotrope can induce fast (≤60 min) and selective removal of non-cellulose components (lignin and hemicellulose) in wood biomass at atmospheric pressure (Bian et al., 2017); (2) the success of producing GQDs from lignin-rich-polysaccharides-few black liquor provides new insights into the sustainable and cost-effectively fabrication of GQDs with high quality (Zhu et al., 2017a); (3) the doped heteroatom(s) can tailor the chemical states and electronic structures of GQDs toward advanced applications (Tetsuka et al., 2016; Li et al., 2012b). Nevertheless, there is rare report on the synthesis of high performance GQDs based on acid hydrotrope fractionation of non-wood plant biomass with much lower recalcitrance, let alone the lack of sufficient mechanistic study associated with their promising applications.

Iron is not only one of the most important trace elements in biological systems, but also a common heavy metal contaminant of industrial effluents and drinking water. Misregulation of iron (particularlly Fe3+) levels in the living cell and water body is concerned with serious diseases and pollution (Li et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2018). Therefore, the sensitive detection of Fe3+ have gained much attention in recent years. Comparing with the traditional colorimetric and electrochemical strategies, fluorescent sensors based on nanoscale GQDs show a great potential due to their lower detection limit, high reliability and faster response (Yan et al., 2019; Memon et al., 2018; Ananthanarayanan et al., 2014). Two alternative mechanisms regulate the specific recognition of Fe3+ by most current GQDs sensors are the static quenching and dynamic quenching, which vary in the interactions between Fe3+ and the sensor (Zhu et al., 2017b). To date, though many good GQDs sensors have already been reported, however, it is still desirable to develop better GQDs with simpler process and greater practicability.

With these considerations in mind, herein, we combine the advantages of both acid hydrotrope method and heteroatom doping to achieve a win-win outcome on the basis of Miscanthus (MC), one of the most widely distributed perennial bioenergy crops. At first, MC was subjected to a mild fractionation by using p-toluenesulfonic acid as hydrotrope. Subsequently, the obtained spent liquor that contained most extracted glucose, xylose and lignin was hydrothermal carbonized with a co-dopant for sulfur amd nitrogen to form the MC-derived GQDs (M-GQDs). Meanwhile the resultant water-insoluble solid was enzymatically hydrolyzed to yield glucose. M-GQDs were late employed as the fluorescent sensor for label‐free detection of Fe3+ ions.

Biomass MC was harvested from Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region in South China. The yielded MC was de-barked before performing twin-screw extrusion and disc milling, then passed through a 20-mesh sieve and air-dried. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. The dialysis bag with a cut-off molecular weight of 1000 Da (Viskase Companies, Inc., USA) was used here. Cellulase (Cellic CTec2) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (China) whose original manufacturer was Novozymes (North America Inc., USA).

p-toluenesulfonic acid (P-acid) was used as hydrotrope in this study. Firstly, a certain amount of P-acid was stirred with distilled water (D-water) and heated at 80 °C until a clear solution was obtained, subsequently the air-dried MC (5% wt/vol) was manually fed into the solution under continuous stirring at 80 °C for 20, 60, or 120 min. Secondly, same volume of D-water as the solution was poured in and immediately vacuum filtered. Thus, MC was fractionated into a lignocellulose-rich water insoluble solid (WIS) in the form of filter cake and a lignin oligomer/monosaccharide mixture in the form of filtrate. The latter was expected to further form GQDs as it had similar compositions as black liquor that was successfully used for fabricating GQDs (Jiang et al., 2019). To facilitate discussion, the reaction conditions were abbreviated as PxxT80tyyy, where xx represented the P-acid concentration (50, 65, or 80 wt%), T represented the reaction temperature as 80 °C, and yyy represented the reaction time (20, 60 or 120 min). In addition, WIS were further enzymatically hydrolyzed as previously reported to investigate their potential as alternative biorefinery feedstock for glucose production (Zhou et al., 2020). The obtained fractionated samples are displayed in Fig. S1.

The collected filtrate (20 mL) was mixed with p-amino-benzenesulfonic acid monosodium salt (C6H6NNaO3S·xH2O, 0.57 g) under continuous ultrasonication for 2 h. Then the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resultant was vacuum filtered through a microporous membrane (0.90 µm, 50 mm), the filtrate and filter cake were then dialyzed against D-water for three days to obtain M-GQDs and biochar, respectively. In final, powdery M-GQDs were yielded (19.8 wt%) after a three-day lyophilization (−85 °C and 5.6 Pa). For comparison purpose, (1) the same dialysis and lyophilization were directly performed on the collected filtrate to yield lignin nanoparticles (LNPs) in solids; (2) the same hydrothermal treatment was performed on the collected filtrate without adding C6H6NNaO3S·xH2O, the thus-obtained resultant was labeled as PG0 in the following discussion.
The compositional analysis of MC and fractionated samples (WIS and spent liquor) was carried out according to the National Renewable Energy Laboratory standard method (Sluiter et al., 2008).

The weight-average and number-average molecular weights (Mw and Mn, respectively) with related polydispersity index (Mw/Mn) of the fractionated LNPs (after acetylation as reported by Li et al. (2018c) were analyzed by GPC (LC-20A, Shimadzu Co., Japan). The eluent was an isocratic 100% tetrahydrofuran (THF, HPLC grade without stabilizer) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min and the column temperature was kept at 30 °C. Each sample in THF (0.5%, wt/vol) was injected after passing through a 0.45 µm filter and then detected using a refractive index detector.

The particle size and crystalline fringe with corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of M-GQDs were studied by using TEM (JEM-2100 UHR, JEOL, Japan) operating at 300 kV. All samples were re-dispersed in ethanol with a concentration of 0.5% (wt/vol) and carefully dripped on the Cu foil. The obtained images were post-analyzed using Image-Pro Plus software.

The topographic morphology of GQDs and LCNF were observed using a Bio-MFP3D AFM (Asylum Research, USA). All samples were re-dispersed in D-water with a concentration of 0.1 wt% and carefully dripped on the freshly cleaved mica sheet for overnight air-drying in advance. The obtained images were post-analyzed using Gwyddion software.

The functional groups of MC, LNPs, and M-GQDs were recorded with a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Vertex 80 V, Bruker Optik GmbH, Germany). All samples were freeze-dried in advance and co-grounded with potassium bromide to form a tablet for the measurements. Raman spectra of samples were observed on a spectrometer (DXR 532, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) equipped with a 532 nm diode laser excitation source. The elemental distributions of M-GQDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using AXIS Ultra DLD (Shimadzu Co., Japan) with a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source and elemental analysis (2400Ⅱ, Perkin Elmer, USA). The lignin substructures of LNPs and M-GQDs were characterized by two-dimensional heteronuclear singular quantum coherence nuclear magnetic resonance (2D HSQC NMR) according to our previous study (Wang et al., 2019).

Optical properties of M-GQDs include the UV–vis spectra and photoluminescence (PL) excitation/emission spectra were acquired using a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Japan) and an FLS 980 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK), respectively. The quantum yield (QY) of M-CQDs at a concentration of 3 mgL–1 is determined using the quinine sulfate (QY=54%) as a standard reference (Xiong et al., 2019). The PL lifetimes studied in this work were measured using a Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France), the obtained data of which were further second-order exceptionally fitted to receive corresponding decay curves. For the pH-dependence study, a desired amount of either hydrochloric acid solution (1 M) or sodium hydroxide solution (1 M) was added to reach the desired pH.

The fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and other metal ions (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Co2+, and Cr2O7 -2) was performed at room temperature. Concentrated metal ion solutions (500 μM) were mixed with a certain amount of M-GQDs to result in a concentration of 3 mgL–1 for the latter and rested for 20 min. The PL excitation/emission spectra of them were then recorded as carefully described above, all measurements were conducted in triplicate. In order to assess the related sensitivity, the theoretical limit of detection (LOD) was introduced and calculated based on the following equation:
L O D =
3 σ
K
where 3 is the signal-to-noise ratio; σ represents the standard deviation obtained at the lowest tested concentration of the used metal ion (0.0023 μM here); K represents the slope of the linearly concentration-dependent response.

To further evaluate the feasibility of M-GQDs-induced fluorometric detection, real water samples that contain Fe3+ of unknown concentrations were used as diluted solvents instead of D-water. The fluorescence experiments were repeated using tap water and Zihuxi Stream water (a stream in the Nanjing Forestry University campus) after the digestion, which was performed by heating the sample (100 mL) with fume nitric acid (5 mL) for 2 h. Thus, all included iron (55.845 g/mole) were oxidized to Fe3+. All tests were repeated in triplicate and the average values were reported. For comparison purpose, inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS, 7700X, Agilent Technologies, Inc., USA) was also applied to acquire the concentration of Fe3+ in the samples.

Since MC is a composite primarily comprising of cellulose, hemicelluloses, and lignin, understanding the effects upon MC with various compositions in response to the fractionation conditions can provide a strategy for future screening and selection or treatment of MC as the starting material for desired end products. As listed in Table 1, the glucan loss for all WIS samples is within 10.8–21.5% in contrast to pristine MC, especially at a reaction time as short as 20 min. Moreover, prominent degradations are also observed in WIS associated with xylan (73.1–95.1%) and lignin (50.2–82.4%). This can be explained by the fact that higher P-acid concentration and longer time can cause severer fractionation, as reported in our previous study (Bian et al., 2020). These results suggest that the fractionation of MC can be realized by retaining most cellulose in WIS while primarily extracting hemicellulose and lignin to the spent liquor. Table 1 also records the GPC-estimated molecular weights. By applying concentrated P-acid solution, both Mw and Mw/Mn for PxxT80t20 LNPs illustrate the downtrend that means a facilitated depolymerization of lignin. However, cooperatively increasing the reaction time conversely leads to lignin condensation as P80T80t60/t120 LNPs present much higher Mw and Mw/Mn. The Mw distribution versus Mw curves in Fig. S2 further confirm the reaction time contribution to Mw. Even though the bimodality is generally observed, however, PxxT80t20 LNPs exhibit narrower Mw range than P80T80t60/t120 LNPs, which is attributed to higher degree of polymeric polydispersity in the former three (Huang et al., 2017a).

Summarily, Scheme 1a displays that P80T80t20 WIS have an acceptable yield (53.8%), limited glucan loss (16.2%), and sharp reduction of xylan (84.0%), while P80T80t20 LNPs have the lowest Mw as well as Mw/Mn. This fractionation condition appears to well equilibrate the cell wall recalcitrance reduction of MC with reltaively retaining more lignocellulose, which is later utilized for further downstream processing. On one hand, a high glucose yield of 87.4% can be readily obtained for P80T80t20 WIS after an enzymatic hydrolysis. On the other hand, the spent liquor is then hydrothermally treated to generate M-GQDs and biochar (Scheme 1b). The chemical changes of lignin substructures between P80T80t20 LNPs and M-GQDs are summarized in Fig. S3, confirming M-GQDs were formed from the former by remaining most guaiacyl units. Biochar is a versatile carbon-rich material that can be prepared from various biomass and organic wastes (Woolf et al., 2010), while M-GQDs may hold great promise for the label‐free fluorescent detection of Fe3+ as discussed later.

The planar and lateral size features of M-GQDs are observed by TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 1, the wide area TEM and AFM images reveal that M-GQDs are built from well-dispersed nanoparticles with uniform size of 4.05 ± 0.61 nm and height of 1.76 ± 0.28 nm. The HR-TEM image in Fig. 1b gives a clear view of well-resolved lattice fringes with a d-spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to that of (100) planes of graphitic carbon (Wang et al., 2014). Inset fast FFT pattern in Fig. 1b further shows a six-fold symmetric hexagon that is similar to graphene (Gu et al., 2009), indicating that graphene-like single crystals are formed in M-GQDs. Also, statistical analysis of the AFM-measured height distribution for M-GQDs (Fig. 1d) exhibits a sharp peak around 1.76 nm, which agrees that M-GQDs are separated well without stacking. Based on the above, we conclude that M-GQDs are graphene-like nanodots, so‐called GQDs.

Fig. 2a displays the FTIR spectra of MC, P80T80t20 LNPs and M-GQDs. Compared with MC, the peaks related to cellulose are either absent (896 cm−1) or changed (2835–2980 cm−1) for P80T80t20 LNPs. Meanwhile, the peak that is ascribed to ether vibrations, methoxyl and β-O-4 (1004–1047 cm−1) disappears, while there are brand new C-O stretching peaks at 1704, 1269 and 1215 cm−1 (Kubo and Kadla, 2005; Shi et al., 2019; Schwanninger et al., 2004). M-GQDs show similar FTIR spectrum as P80T80t20 LNPs, except for the newly presented C-N stretching peak at 1400 cm−1 and asymmetric stretching vibrations of C-S at 756 and 690 cm−1 (Qu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2019). These results indicated that: (1) the chemical nature of P80T80t20 LNPs as lignin and the ether cleavage of lignin in MC by acid hydrotrope fractionation; (2) the sulfur, nitrogen (S, N) co-doping of M-GQDs by hydrothermal treatment with C6H6NNaO3S·xH2O. In addition, the presence of abundant polar functional groups in M-GQDs implies that they may have good water dispersity. The detailed FTIR peak assignments are listed in Table S1. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the relative intensities of the disordered D peak/ordered G (ID/IG) shown in the Raman spectra of PG0 and M-GQDs are 0.86 and 1.09, respectively, indicating the regular carbon structure of them is similar to graphite. The higher ID/IG ratio of M-GQDs over PG0 also suggests more structural defects in M-GQDs than in the latter. Moreover, it was already reported that a moderate ID/IG ratio of 1.33 was related to high QY of 30.3% for carbon nanodots (Liu et al., 2020). This is similar to what observed here as the QY of M-GQDs with the ID/IG ratio of 1.09 is 20.2%, in contrast to 2.5% of PG0 (ID/IG =0.86).

XPS analysis is also carried out to determine the chemical composition of M-GQDs. Fig. 2c exhibits the XPS survey spectra of M-GQDs that have four prominent peaks at 168.0, 284.8, 400.4, and 532.6 eV, which are assigned to S 2p (2.83 wt%), C 1s (68.43 wt%), N 1s (2.53 wt%), and O 1s (26.21 wt%), respectively. These observations are consistent with the corresponding FTIR results that M-GQDs are co-doped with sulfur and nitrogen. The related high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 2d–g) can be deconvoluted into the following peaks: (1) S-C at 164.0 eV and SOx-C (x = 2 or 3) that includes two peaks at 167.6 and 168.4 eV; (2) C-C, C-S, C-N, C-O, and C˭O at 284.5, 285.1, 285.5, 286.2, and 288.9 eV, respectively; (3) -NH2 at 400.4 eV and N-C at 402.0 eV; (4) 531.2 eV for O˭C and at 532.6 eV for O-C (Wang et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2014). Note, sulfurous and amino groups can serve as electron acceptor and surface-passivation agent to induce strong intramolecular charge transfer and improved emission efficiency, respectively (Wang et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2016). In other words, the S, N co-doped M-GQDs are expected to present excellent PL performances, which is also supported by the results shown in Fig. S4. The elemental results listed in Table S2 qualitatively confirm that M-GQDs are S, N co-doped.

The UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and PL emission spectra are applied to understand the optical uniqueness of M-GQDs ( Fig. 3a). Visually, M-GQDs are clear and transparent in lemon yellow (inset Ⅰ) under daylight unless excited under 365 nm UV to emit cyan fluorescence (inset Ⅱ and Fig. 3b). There are two UV shoulder peaks centered at 255 nm and 310 nm, which are attributed to the characteristic peak of lignin monomers and n-π* of the surface groups, respectively (Musha and Goring, 1975; Liu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2016). Besides, two prominent PL excitation peaks located at 358 and 408 nm, accordingly inducing two PL emission peaks at 494 (with a QY of 20.2%) and 499 nm. It is also noted that the UV–vis absorption peak of M-GQDs appears at the wavelength close to that of their PL excitation peak, indicating that there is almost no nonradioactive transition in M-GQDs (Yuan et al., 2016). Further, Fig. 3c shows that M-GQDs possess a typical excitation-dependent PL emission, of which the emissive peaks are gradually red-shifted with increasing the excitation wavelength from 280 nm to 500 nm with an increment of 20 nm. These results demonstrate that there exists two excitation bands in M-GQDs, due to the co-doped sulfur and nitrogen can synergistically change the chemical environment of M-GQDs (Qu et al., 2015). Based on this, it is clear that the energy transfer in M-GQDs varies under different PL excitation wavelengths, which is a prerequisite for many optical applications. The lifetime test further confirms this as a relatively long lifetime (τave) of 11.95 ns is observed for M-GQDs, as displayed in Fig. 3d (Zhang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ability of M-GQDs to detect Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: As per aforementioned rationales, it is worth exploring the potential of M-GQDs as a fluorescent platform to sensitively monitor Fe3+. Fig. 4a shows that M-GQDs fluoresce well over a wide pH range from 3 to 11 regarding their PL emission/excitation spectrum excited under 358 nm (Em 358 nm/Ex 358 nm) or PL emission spectrum excited under 408 nm (Em 408 nm). But all observed PL intensities decrease with increasing pH because sulfurous groups can lead to decreased fluorescence in alkali environment, while the N-induced fluorescence enhancement is also attenuated at high pH (Li et al., 2012b). Moreover, a further decrease in the PL intensities is observed in Fig. 4b upon additionally adding in 500 μM of Fe3+. This demonstrates that Fe3+, a well-known trace element and pollutant, may impose fluorescence quenching to M-GQDs. Thus, we speculated that the S, N co-doped M-GQDs may be employed as the fluorescent sensor of Fe3+. The camera images shown in Fig. 4c also support this. In addition, the related time-dependent fluorescence study reveals that M-GQDs are highly photo-stable at pH 7 (Fig. S5), thus the neutral condition (pH 7) was chosen for the subsequent investigations.

Fig. 4d–f further exhibit the PL measurements of M-GQDs within a wide concentration range of Fe3+ (0, 0.0023, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10.6, 25, 50, 112, 175, 250, 325, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000, 2500, and 3000 μM). Apparently, M-GQDs are sensitive to Fe3+ as their PL intensity continues to decrease with increasing Fe3+ concentration. Intriguingly, this phenomenon is repeatedly observed in all three spectra include Em 358 nm, Em 408 nm and Ex 358 nm, potentially rendering M-GQDs be available for a tri-channel detection of Fe3+. This is attributed to the multiple PL-centers in M-GQDs can cause energy transitions between bandgaps, which also accounts for their unique response to Fe3+ under varied PL measurement conditions (Park et al., 2020). To further assess the quenching effect of Fe3+ on M-GQDs, the Stern-Volmer equation is employed here:

where F
0 and F are the PL intensities of M-GQDs at Em 358 nm/Em 408 nm/Ex 358 nm in the absence and presence of the PL quencheable Fe3+, respectively; K
SV is the quenching constant of Fe3+, Lmol−1; c is the concentration of added Fe3+, molL −1. The corresponding Stern-Volmer plots within 0–3000 μM of Fe3+ are shown in Fig. S6. It is clear that there are better linear correlations at narrower concentration range composed of two consecutive sections: (1) 0–10.6 μM, high correlation coefficient (R
2 ) as 0.990, 0.989, and 0.991 were observed, along with the K
SV of 2911.13, 2988.19, and 2716.50 Lmol−1 (Fig. 4g–i); (2) 10.6–900/800/1000 μM, high R
2 as 0.995, 0.988, and 0.991 were observed, along with the K
SV of 445.30, 732.98, and 635.17 Lmol−1 (Fig. 4j–l). Accordingly, the LOD can reach as low as 1.41 nM, 2.31 nM and 2.09 nM, respectively. All these LOD values are lower than 1/1000 of the allowed maximum tolerance of iron (3.6 μM/200 μgL−1) according to the newest EU drinking water quality directive 98/83/EC (EU, 1998). Besides, beyond these linear sections, much lower R
2 values are obtained for channel Em 358 (0.963, within 900–3000 μM of Fe3+), channel Em 408 (0.960, within 800–3000 μM of Fe3+) and channel Ex 358 (0.938, within 1000–3000 μM of Fe3+), as displayed in Fig. S6. It is noteworthy that M-GQDs outperform most previously reported carbon dots and GQDs for Fe3+ detection regarding the LOD and linear range ( Table 2). To further evaluate the practical potential of M-GQDs, the tri-channel fluorescence experiments were then repeated by using real water samples that contain Fe3+ of unknown concentrations. By comparing with those results determined by ICP-MS (Table S3), it is clear that comparable accuracy is obtained for M-GQDs, especially the Ex 358 nm channel. Therefore, it is concluded that M-GQDs are promising to be employed in real environmental quantification of Fe3+ upon further progression.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of M-GQDs to detect Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching is usually explained by the collisional or dynamic quenching mechanism. Fig. 5a shows the time-resolved PL decay curves of M-GQDs mixed with different concentrations of Fe3+, the monitored τave decreases from 11.95 to 9.98 ns as the concentration increases from 0 to 500 μM. Thus it is obvious that the observed fluorescence quenching is originated from the dynamic quenching dominated by collisional deactivation rather than complexation (Wang et al., 2014). Given that M-GQDs are decorated with amino groups, these electron donors can combine Fe3+ to induce considerable energy dissipation instead of fluorescence (Liu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2014), as schematically shown in Fig. 5b. To have a deeper understand of this, we further perform the PL measurements in the presence of other metal ions involve Ag+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Co2+, and Cr2O2–7 and the results are shown in Fig. 5c–e. There is no comparable fluorescence quenching as noted for Fe3+ except Cr2O2–7, which can be explained as follows: (1) Fe3+ has stronger binding affinity than other metal ions towards the electron-donating groups in M-GQDs, leading to faster charge transfer and restrained recombination of excitons to induce sharper decline of PL intensity (Xu et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2020); (2) Cr2O2–7 is much more active than Fe3+ and can enable rapider electron transfer and thus improves the collisional process to result in more significant fluorescence reduction (Olley et al., 2011); (3) the introduction of Fe3+ may accelerate the consumption of reductive photogenerated electron, hence less electron-withdrawing sulfurous groups will be activated for the PL enhancement (Huang et al., 2017b). In order to confirm the proposed mechanism, we further applied XPS and FTIR spectra to fully characterize the elemental composition, chemical state and structures of M-GQDs added with Fe3+. As displayed in Fig. S7, there is no peak shift observed in the XPS survey, N 1s XPS spectra or FTIR spectra of M-GQDs after the interaction with Fe3+. These results again confirmed that this study was based on the non-complexation mechanism unlike the previously published works (Wu et al., 2014; He et al., 2020).

In conclusion, we reported a highly fluorescent GQDs from biorefinery waste for tri-channel sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions. Acid hydrotrope fractionation allows selective removal of xylan (up to 95.1%) and lignin (up to 82.4%) from MC biomass, with subsequent hydrothermal treatment to form single crystalline GQDs of S, N co-doping. M-GQDs exhibit a linear fluorescent response towards trace amount of Fe3+ that has rarely been reported, and even provide a comparable determination of Fe3+ in real-environment water. Further investigations prove that this is based on a dynamic quenching mechanism due to a synergistic effect: (1) the decorated electron-donating amino groups can combine Fe3+ to induce additional energy dissipation; (2) Fe3+ may compete with the sulfurous groups in M-GQDs for binding photogenerated electrons, decreasing the activation of the latter that accounts for PL enhancement. Summarily, M-GQDs are highly promising to serve as a fluorometric platform for the highly sensitive and accurate determination of Fe3+ in aqueous media.
